action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: king, bird, hunter, farmer, dog, hill, spear, lake, road, boat, tree, stone, river, ship
Adjectives: big, red, cold, blue, small, tall, old, dark
Verbs (write them in this base form): see, chase, feed, find, watch, hold, carry, hear
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three, four, five, six (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three, four, five, six) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
- zero or more adjectives may come before the noun.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a king see a bird
    six kings see a bird
    a big king see a bird

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    the five lakes carry the big farmer  =>  gugepa fugo fogupo getupopa ti
    five hunters watch the two red ships  =>  gisapopa fago te kutopi sukupo ti
    a blue road feed the five farmers  =>  gugepopa ti totu tigo lofa
    a cold road find three ships  =>  gisapo pi kutofo tigo gugefe
    the four hunters find the three big rivers  =>  gesufapopa fugo pi kutofo sukupopa se
    the road find three red roads  =>  tigopo fago pi kutofo tigopa
    the two boats chase the four kings  =>  logipopa se tuli gasitipopa te
    three stones find the two old trees  =>  palepopa giso te kutofo gulupo pi
    three farmers watch the six rivers  =>  gesufapopa pufa kutopi gugepo pi
    the two rivers watch a tall hill  =>  kugi gole kutopi gesufapopa te
    the two hills feed three dark dogs  =>  fapopo lulo pi totu kugipopa te
    the five birds carry six rivers  =>  gesufapo pufa fogupo kafepopa ti
    the four dark boats hold four dark spears  =>  kasefupo lulo se gile gasitipopa lulo se
    the two lakes hear a dark hill  =>  kugi lulo fosu getupopa te
    two small birds hear a dog  =>  fapo fosu kafepo lesa te
    a hill see a tall river  =>  gesufa gole kake kugi

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 5 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 4 English sentences (so at most
   20 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 4 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
PROBES:
1. a king see a bird
2. the five lakes carry the big farmer
3. five hunters watch the two red ships
4. the four hunters find the three big rivers
action.label: response

probes: the five lakes carry the big farmer
five hunters watch the two red ships
the four hunters find the three big rivers

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a king see a bird"  =>  kafe kake logi
2. "the five lakes carry the big farmer"  =>  gugepa fugo fogupo getupopa ti
3. "five hunters watch the two red ships"  =>  gisapopa fago te kutopi sukupo ti
4. "the four hunters find the three big rivers"  =>  gesufapopa fugo pi kutofo sukupopa se
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 5 done. Probes used: 4 of 20. 4 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

Adjectives can appear before the noun. The language uses a single verb for "carry" (fugo) and "find" (pi). The language uses a single verb for "watch" (fago). FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. the river hear six tall lakes
2. a farmer feed a king
3. two dark birds watch the two red hunters
4. three dogs chase five big farmers
5. five birds find the three hunters
6. five ships chase six stones
7. a river watch the two spears
8. the four red trees hear six spears
9. the five dogs feed five cold spears
10. the three roads watch two hills

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to translate 10 sentences into the target language based on the grammar rules I've learned. The language follows SVO order, uses articles 'the' and 'a' for definite and indefinite singular nouns, and number words set the count. Adjectives can appear before nouns. The verbs are: see (kake), chase (tuli), feed (totu), find (pi), watch (fago), hold (lulo), carry (fugo), hear (fosu). The language uses a single verb for each action. I'll translate each sentence carefully.

ANSWERS:
1. kugi lulo fosu kake se kutofo ti
2. totu kafe logi
3. kafepo fosu fago te kutopi sukupo ti
4. tuli kutofo fugo pi kutofo fapopo ti
5. pi kutofo fago fapo ti
6. tuli kutofo se kutofo se kutofo se
7. fago kugi se gasitipopa ti
8. kutofo se fosu kutofo se kutofo se gasitipopa ti
9. totu kutofo fosu kutofo se kutofo se
